evidence.quote_or_summary: There are two great seasons to do the Buran Ghati trek — summer and autumn.
Buran Ghati in Summer (mid-May to end of June)
Buran Ghati in Autumn (mid-September to mid-October)

values: Summer: Buran Pass is a dramatic wall of ice in June; rappelling down an ice wall while crossing the pass
Summer: Vast snowfields from Dhunda till the pass; ~150 m rappel from the top of the pass; trekking in snow for around an hour after crossing the pass; multiple snow slides
Summer: Snow starts melting in latter part of June; snow close to the pass may not be enough for big slides
Summer: Trail changes from white to greens and yellows; wildflowers bloom on meadows and around campsites; grasslands turn bright green with bright yellow flowers
Autumn: Fiery, glorious colours on the trail; shades of green near base camp and through forests/grasslands; yellows, oranges, and browns closer to the pass

evidence.quote_or_summary: Summer is a coveted season to do the Buran Ghati trek because the Buran Pass itself is a dramatic wall of ice in June. So, you get to rappel down an ice wall while crossing the famed pass.

imagine vast snowfields starting from Dhunda till the pass. Then, you rappel down from the top of the pass for about a 150 m section. Even after crossing the pass, you trek in snow for around an hour. Multiple snow slides will have you squeaking with almost childish delight.
This snow starts melting in the latter part of June. So, while there will be snow close to the pass, it may not be enough to offer you big slides.

The colour of the trail also starts changing shades of white to greens and yellows as you move from May to June. Wildflowers start blooming on the meadows and around the campsites. The grasslands turn a bright shade of green interspersed with bright yellow flowers.
Despite this, autumn is one of the best times to do the Buran Ghati trek as you get to see the fiery, glorious colours during this time.

Initially, you’ll notice many shades of green near the base camp. This continues as you trek through forests and grasslands. But as you gain altitude and closer to the pass, you’ll notice more yellows, oranges, and browns.

You see snow but in smaller patches. The ice on the Buran Ghati wall has melted away, exposing rocky terrain. You trek down this rocky patch while descending. There’s no rappelling. This makes the trek a bit easier in the autumn.
By October, there are chances of witnessing the first winter snow of the year.

values: May: snow at Litham campsite

Latter part of June: snow melting; snow close to pass may not be enough for big slides
Autumn: snow in smaller patches; frost at Dhunda campsite; chances of snowfall at Buran Ghati Pass when weather goes bad; chances of first winter snow by October

evidence.quote_or_summary: You will find snow at the Litham campsite in May. During June, you can expect snow at the last alpine campsite of Dhunda.

imagine vast snowfields starting from Dhunda till the pass. Then, you rappel down from the top of the pass for about a 150 m section. Even after crossing the pass, you trek in snow for around an hour.
This snow starts melting in the latter part of June. So, while there will be snow close to the pass, it may not be enough to offer you big slides.
You can expect frost at the campsite of Dhunda. There are chances of snowfall at the Buran Ghati Pass when weather goes bad.
By October, there are chances of witnessing the first winter snow of the year.
